title: Transformers & Attention
eli8:
# Paying Attention Like a Detective 🔦

Imagine reading this sentence: *"The bank by the river was muddy."*

When you read the word **"bank"**, how do you know it means a riverbank and not a money bank? You look at **"river"**! Your brain pays *attention* to the right word to understand the meaning.

This is exactly what a **Transformer** does — it lets every word pay attention to every other word at the same time!

## The Attention Spotlight 🔦

Think of attention like a spotlight:

- When processing the word **"bank"**, the model shines its spotlight on the word **"river"** (very bright!) and a little on **"muddy"** (dim), and almost nothing on **"the"** (off)
- When processing **"muddy"**, the spotlight shines bright on **"river"** and **"bank"
- Every word gets its own spotlight, shining on all the other words!

This is why transformers are so good at understanding context — they can connect words that are far apart in a sentence.

## Why Are Transformers Special? 🚀

Before transformers (around 2017), AI models had to read text like you read a book — one word at a time, left to right. If the important clue was at the beginning of a very long sentence, the model would "forget" it by the end.

Transformers solve this by looking at **all words at once**! It's like reading a whole page in one glance instead of word by word.

## Many Spotlights at Once 💡

Transformers actually have **multiple attention heads** — imagine 8 or 16 spotlights, each looking for different things:

- 🔍 One spotlight looks for **who is doing what** (subject-verb relationships)
- 🏷️ Another looks for **what describes what** (adjective-noun pairs)
- 📍 Another tracks **positions and order**

Together, they paint a rich picture of what the text means! That's the magic of multi-head attention.
detail:
# Transformers & Attention

## The Attention Mechanism

The transformer attention mechanism, introduced in "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al., 2017), computes relationships between all positions in a sequence simultaneously.

For each position, three vectors are computed from the input embedding:
- **Query (Q)**: What am I looking for?
- **Key (K)**: What information do I contain?
- **Value (V)**: What information will I contribute?

**Scaled dot-product attention**:
```
Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QKᵀ / √dₖ) · V
```

The scaling factor `√dₖ` (where dₖ is the key dimension) prevents dot products from growing large enough to push softmax into regions of extremely small gradients.

```python
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, mask=None):
    d_k = Q.size(-1)
    scores = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / (d_k ** 0.5)
    if mask is not None:
        scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float('-inf'))
    attn_weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
    return torch.matmul(attn_weights, V), attn_weights
```

## Multi-Head Attention

Instead of one attention function, multi-head attention runs h parallel attention operations ("heads") on linearly projected versions of Q, K, V:

```
MultiHead(Q, K, V) = Concat(head₁, ..., headₕ) Wᴼ
where headᵢ = Attention(Q Wᵢᑫ, K Wᵢᴷ, V Wᵢᵛ)
```

Each head learns to attend to different types of relationships:
- Syntactic dependencies (subject-verb)
- Coreference (pronoun to antecedent)
- Positional patterns
- Semantic similarity

GPT-2 has 12 heads; GPT-3 has 96 heads per layer.

## The Transformer Block

A single transformer block contains:

1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention**: Each token attends to all other tokens
2. **Residual connection**: Add input back to attention output (stabilizes training)
3. **Layer Normalization**: Normalize activations (before or after, depending on architecture)
4. **Feed-Forward Network**: 2-layer MLP applied independently to each position
5. **Another residual + LayerNorm**

Modern LLMs stack 12–96+ of these blocks (GPT-2: 12, GPT-3: 96, LLaMA 3 70B: 80).

## Positional Encoding

Since attention is permutation-invariant (it treats tokens as a set, not a sequence), positional information must be injected explicitly.

**Sinusoidal positional encoding** (original paper): Fixed sin/cos functions at different frequencies
**Learned absolute positional embeddings**: Trainable position vectors (GPT-2, GPT-3)
**RoPE** (Rotary Position Embedding): Relative positions encoded in attention (LLaMA, Mistral) — see Topic 9

## Encoder vs Decoder vs Encoder-Decoder

**Encoder-only (BERT, RoBERTa)**:
- Bidirectional attention: each token sees all other tokens
- Used for classification, NER, semantic search
- Cannot generate text autoregressively

**Decoder-only (GPT family, LLaMA, Claude)**:
- Causal (masked) attention: each token sees only previous tokens
- Naturally suited to autoregressive text generation
- The dominant architecture for modern LLMs

**Encoder-decoder (T5, BART)**:
- Encoder processes input with full attention
- Decoder generates output attending to encoder states + its own past
- Used for translation, summarization

## Why Transformers Replaced RNNs

**Parallelism during training**: RNNs must process tokens sequentially (token N depends on N-1). Transformers compute all positions simultaneously, enabling efficient GPU parallelism.

**Long-range dependencies**: RNN hidden states degrade over long sequences. Attention directly connects any two positions in O(1) steps (vs O(n) for RNNs).

**Scaling**: Transformers scale predictably — more parameters + more data = better performance (Chinchilla scaling laws).

mcqs.question: In the attention mechanism, what do the Query, Key, and Value vectors represent conceptually?
options: Query = what I'm looking for, Key = what I contain/offer, Value = information I contribute when attended to
Query = the current token, Key = the previous token, Value = the next token
Query = the encoder output, Key = the decoder input, Value = the attention weight
Query = word frequency, Key = word position, Value = word embedding
mcqs.explanation: In attention, the Query asks 'what am I looking for?', the Key signals 'what information do I have?', and the Value is the actual information contributed when a high-attention match is found between a query and a key.

mcqs.question: Why is the dot product in scaled dot-product attention divided by √dₖ?
options: To ensure the output values are always between 0 and 1
To prevent dot products from growing large and pushing softmax into regions with vanishing gradients
To normalize by the sequence length
To account for different vocabulary sizes
mcqs.explanation: As the dimension dₖ grows, dot products grow proportionally in magnitude, causing softmax to produce extremely peaked distributions (near-zero gradients). Dividing by √dₖ counteracts this scaling effect.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of multiple attention heads in multi-head attention?
options: To process different parts of the input sequence in parallel to reduce computation time
To allow the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces, capturing different types of relationships
To handle inputs in multiple languages simultaneously
To double-check the attention weights for accuracy
mcqs.explanation: Each attention head operates on linearly projected subspaces and learns to attend to different relationship types (syntactic, semantic, positional, coreference). The outputs are concatenated and projected, combining these different perspectives.

mcqs.question: What distinguishes an encoder-only model (like BERT) from a decoder-only model (like GPT)?
options: BERT uses more attention heads than GPT
BERT uses bidirectional attention (sees all tokens), making it suited for understanding; GPT uses causal attention (sees only past tokens), suited for generation
BERT can only process short texts; GPT handles long contexts
GPT uses sinusoidal positional encoding; BERT uses learned embeddings
mcqs.explanation: Encoder-only models use bidirectional (full) self-attention — every token attends to every other. This captures rich context for classification/understanding tasks but prevents autoregressive generation. Decoder-only models use causal masking so each token only sees previous tokens, enabling next-token prediction.

mcqs.question: Why did transformers replace RNNs as the dominant language model architecture?
options: Transformers use less memory than RNNs during inference
RNNs cannot handle vocabulary sizes above 50,000 tokens
Transformers compute all positions in parallel during training, avoiding RNNs' sequential bottleneck and handling long-range dependencies better
Transformers do not require backpropagation, making training faster
mcqs.explanation: RNNs process tokens sequentially (each step depends on the previous), preventing GPU parallelism and causing gradients to vanish over long sequences. Transformers attend to all positions simultaneously, fully exploiting GPU parallelism and connecting distant tokens in a single layer.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the residual connection in a transformer block?
options: To skip certain layers for sparse computation
To add the layer's input back to its output, preserving gradient flow and allowing the network to learn incremental refinements
To connect the encoder output to the decoder input
To regularize the attention weights by adding random noise
mcqs.explanation: Residual (skip) connections add the input of a sub-layer to its output: output = input + SubLayer(input). This preserves gradient flow through deep networks (solving vanishing gradient) and allows each layer to learn residual corrections rather than full transformations.

mcqs.question: How many transformer layers does GPT-3 have?
mcqs.explanation: GPT-3 (175B parameters) has 96 transformer layers. For comparison, GPT-2 has 12 layers (small) to 48 layers (XL), and LLaMA 3 70B has 80 layers.

flashcards.back: An attention mechanism where a sequence attends to itself — each position in the sequence computes attention over all other positions. Enables the model to relate tokens to each other regardless of distance.

Three learned linear projections of token embeddings used in attention. Q asks 'what am I looking for?', K signals 'what do I contain?', V is 'what information I provide'. Attention score = softmax(QKᵀ/√dₖ) · V.

Running h parallel attention operations on linearly projected Q, K, V subspaces, then concatenating and projecting the results. Each 'head' learns to attend to different relationship types (syntax, semantics, position).

A mechanism for injecting sequential position information into the transformer, which is otherwise permutation-invariant. Approaches: sinusoidal (original paper), learned absolute embeddings (GPT-2), RoPE (LLaMA).
The repeating unit of a transformer: Multi-Head Self-Attention → Add & LayerNorm → Feed-Forward Network → Add & LayerNorm. Modern LLMs stack 12–96+ of these blocks.

A transformer component using bidirectional (full) self-attention — every token attends to every other token. Used in BERT for understanding/classification tasks. Cannot generate text autoregressively.
A transformer component using causal (masked) self-attention — each token can only attend to previous tokens. The architecture used by GPT, LLaMA, Claude. Enables autoregressive text generation.

An attention mask applied in decoder-only transformers that sets future-position attention scores to -∞ before softmax, preventing each token from 'seeing' future tokens during training and generation.

videos.title: The Illustrated Transformer — explained visually
videos.description: A highly visual walkthrough of the complete transformer architecture: encoder, decoder, multi-head attention, positional encoding, and how all components connect. Based on Jay Alammar's classic blog post.

videos.title: Transformer Neural Networks, ChatGPT's foundation — StatQuest
videos.description: StatQuest's accessible step-by-step explanation of the transformer, building intuition for attention weights and why transformers are so effective at modeling sequential data.
